When a sinner becomes a true believer, a significant change occurs in their state, regardless of what they have done previously. Because they are justified by faith, they experience peace with God. A holy and righteous God cannot be at peace with a sinner while that person is under the guilt of sin. Justification removes this guilt, creating an opportunity for peace. This happens through our Lord Jesus Christ; he is the great peacemaker, the mediator between God and man. The blessed state of believers is one of grace. We are brought into this grace, which teaches us that we were not born in such a state. We could not have entered it ourselves, but we are led into it as pardoned offenders. In this state, we stand firm; this posture signifies perseverance, and we remain safe, upheld by God’s power. Those who hope for the glory of God in the future have ample reason to rejoice now. Suffering produces patience, not on its own, but through the powerful grace of God working within it. Those who suffer patiently receive abundant divine comfort. This suffering provides valuable experience about ourselves. This hope will not disappoint because it is sealed with the Holy Spirit as a spirit of love. It is the gracious work of the blessed Spirit to spread abroad the love of God in the hearts of all believers. A true understanding of God’s love for us will prevent us from being ashamed, either of our hope or of our suffering for him.

Christ died for sinners; not only those who were useless, but also those who were guilty and hateful—those whose eternal destruction would glorify God's justice. Christ died to save us, not in our sins, but from our sins; and we were still sinners when he died for us. The carnal mind is not only an enemy of God but is enmity itself. But God intended to deliver people from sin and to bring about a great change. As long as the sinful state continues, God loathes the sinner, and the sinner loathes God. It is a mystery that Christ should die for such people; no other instance of love like this is known, so it may well take eternity to adore and wonder at it. Again, what idea did the apostle have when he considered someone dying for an innocent person? He only presented it as something that might be possible. Was it not because this suffering was endured that the person who would benefit could be released from it? But from what are believers in Christ released by his death? Not from bodily death, because everyone must endure that. The evil from which deliverance could be achieved only in this astonishing manner must be more dreadful than natural death. There is no evil to which this argument can apply except that which the apostle actually affirms: sin and wrath, the punishment for sin, determined by God's unerring justice. And if, through divine grace, they were brought to repent and believe in Christ, and thus were justified by his bloodshedding and by faith in that atonement, much more would they be kept from falling under the power of sin and Satan or departing finally from him. The living Lord of all will complete the purpose of his dying love by saving all true believers to the uttermost. Having such a pledge of salvation in God’s love through Christ, the apostle declared that believers not only rejoiced in the hope of heaven but even in their sufferings for Christ's sake; they also glorified God as their unchanging Friend and all-sufficient Portion, through Christ alone.

The purpose of what follows is clear. It is to elevate our understanding of the blessings Christ has secured for us by comparing them with the evil that followed the fall of our first father; and by showing that these blessings not only remove those evils but extend far beyond. When Adam sinned, his nature became guilty and corrupted, and this passed on to his children. Thus, all have sinned in him. Death comes through sin, because death is the wages of sin. Then entered all that misery which is the deserved consequence of sin: temporal, spiritual, eternal death. If Adam had not sinned, he would not have died; but a sentence of death was passed upon him as a criminal; it passed through all men, like an infectious disease from which no one escapes. In proof of our union with Adam and our part in his first transgression, observe that sin prevailed in the world for many ages before the giving of the law by Moses. Death reigned during that long time, not only over adults who willfully sinned but also over multitudes of infants, which shows that they had fallen in Adam under condemnation, and that Adam’s sin extended to all his descendants. He was a figure or type of Him who was to come as Surety of a new covenant for all who are related to Him.

Through one man's offense, all mankind is exposed to eternal condemnation. But the grace and mercy of God, and the free gift of righteousness and salvation, are through Jesus Christ, as man; yet the Lord from heaven has brought many believers into a more safe and exalted state than that from which they fell in Adam. This free gift did not place them anew in a state of trial but fixed them in a state of justification, as Adam would have been placed had he remained upright. Despite the differences, there is a striking similarity. Just as sin and death prevailed to condemn all men through one person's offense, so grace prevailed to justify all who are related to Christ by faith through the righteousness of one person. Through the grace of God, the gift by grace has been abundant to many through Christ; yet many choose to remain under the dominion of sin and death rather than seek the blessings of the reign of grace. But Christ will not cast out anyone who is willing to come to him.

Through Christ and his righteousness, we have more and greater privileges than we lost through Adam's offense. The moral law showed that many thoughts, dispositions, words, and actions were sinful, thus transgressions were multiplied. This did not cause sin to abound more but revealed its sinfulness, just as letting in clearer light into a room reveals the dust and filth that were there before but were not seen. Adam’s sin and the effect of corruption within us are the source of the abundance of offense that appeared when the law was introduced. The terrors of the law make gospel comforts more sweet. Thus God, through the Holy Spirit, has delivered to us a most important truth full of consolation, suited to our need as sinners. Whatever one may have above another, every person is a sinner against God, stands condemned by the law, and needs pardon. A righteousness that justifies cannot be a mixture of sin and holiness. There can be no claim to an eternal reward without a pure and spotless righteousness; let us look for it, even to the righteousness of Christ.
